exemplar.title: How can we reconcile the relentless drive to pursue knowledge with the finite resources we have available? Discuss with reference to the natural sciences and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: How can we reconcile the relentless drive to pursue knowledge with the finite resources we have available? Discuss with reference to the natural sciences and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and clear focus on the prescribed title, consistently returning to the tension between unlimited knowledge pursuit and finite resources
Effective treatment of both natural sciences and human sciences with well-chosen real-world examples (ITER, Galileo, Unit 731, Boisguilbert)
Coherent, well-organised arguments structured around themes of collaboration, ethics and political context
Demonstrated awareness and evaluation of differing perspectives, weighing scientific progress against ethical and institutional constraints
Precise definition of key terms and a strong conceptual foundation that underpins the entire discussion
weaknesses: Occasional lapses into extended narrative description with limited analytical unpacking of the underlying knowledge questions
Evaluation of perspectives remains at the level of asserting dilemmas rather than deeply probing epistemic assumptions
Transitions between examples can be abrupt, and the concluding synthesis does not fully integrate all strands into a single, overarching resolution
Some examples (e.g., Galileo, Unit 731) would benefit from deeper methodological and ethical analysis to strengthen the critical evaluation
The conclusion summarizes themes well but stops short of offering a definitive, synthesized answer to the prescribed title
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With an official total of 9/10 to allocate and given the essayâs overall high level of coherence, breadth of real-world examples, and clear engagement with multiple perspectives, a near-top mark is warranted. The studentâs strong organisation, precise definitions, and effective use of cases demonstrate performance above the 7â8 band. Minor narrative digressions, occasional under-analysis of methodological and ethical assumptions, and a less integrated conclusion prevent a perfect 10. Awarding 9 reflects the essayâs sustained focus, depth of examples and clear evaluative comments while acknowledging areas for deeper critical engagement and tighter synthesis.

annotations.comment: An explicit statement of the knowledge question early in this introduction would strengthen coherence by clearly linking definitions to the essayâs analytical aims.
The choice of ITER as a real-world case effectively grounds the natural sciences discussion in a contemporary, concrete example of large-scale collaboration.
The discussion of political dynamics and resource hierarchies within ITER shows a nuanced awareness of how non-scientific factors influence knowledge production.
The Galileo example effectively illustrates institutional constraints on epistemic freedom and links well to the human sciences.
The focus on Galileoâs recantation is largely descriptive; more analysis of his methodological innovations and evidential reasoning would deepen the evaluation.
Consider critiquing the reliability of indirect argumentation under duress as a reconciliation method for knowledge production in restrictive contexts.
This example would be stronger with an explicit connection back to how the finite resource of freedom of expression is reconciled in modern scientific communities.
The paragraph conflates dissemination constraints with validity without unpacking how institutional suppression affects the justification of knowledge claims.
This section could be strengthened by explicitly comparing ethical reconciliation methods between natural and human sciences to highlight disciplinary contrasts.
The narrative remains heavy; deeper analysis of the theoretical underpinnings of laissez-faire as an economic knowledge claim would enhance rigor.
Consider evaluating how illegal publication impacted the credibility, dissemination, and long-term acceptance of Boisguilbertâs economic ideas.
This comparative insight would benefit from synthesis: clarify the differing definitions of âfinite resourcesâ across the two areas of knowledge.

The student effectively restates the prescribed title in their own words, demonstrating clear understanding of the task and framing the central tension between resource finitude and knowledge pursuit.
The essay would benefit from an explicit formulation of a knowledge question to focus the discussion and guide analytical responses throughout the essay.
The definitions of key terms such as âreconcileâ and âfinite resourceâ are articulated precisely, establishing a solid conceptual foundation for the essay.
The broad extension of âfinite resourcesâ risks diluting analytical focus; consider narrowing to two or three core types (e.g., financial vs. human capital) for deeper examination.
This narrative description of ITER lacks clear linkage to the underlying knowledge question and does not unpack the epistemic assumptions behind international collaboration.
To strengthen the analysis, discuss how pooling diverse national research affects the reliability and validity of scientific knowledge claims.
This paragraph stops short of evaluating how political influence shapes the objectivity of scientific results; consider exploring specific cases of bias in decision-making.
The transition from technical collaboration to the opportunity cost of ITER is abrupt; explicitly tie this back to the central knowledge question for coherence.
The Boisguilbert case is a nuanced choice in the human sciences, illustrating institutional censorship and the emergence of economic ideas.
The reflection on Galileoâs legacy and the evolution of knowledge across shifting contexts shows an appreciation for the dynamic nature of scientific understanding.
It would benefit from explicitly discussing how changing definitions of âfinite resourcesâ influence the epistemic risks taken by knowers.
Using Unit 731 as an example powerfully foregrounds the ethical pitfalls of treating human life as a resource in knowledge production.
This atrocity description remains largely narrative; a critical engagement with ethical frameworks (e.g., utilitarian vs. deontological) would add depth.
Examine how dehumanization as a resource choice undermines the reliability and legitimacy of the knowledge claims generated.
Propose alternative reconciliation methods for human sciences research that respect ethical boundaries while still advancing understanding.
The studentâs acknowledgement of long-term ethical implications demonstrates an awareness of how past methods influence future knowledge practices.
The conclusion effectively summarizes the main examples and highlights the essayâs key themes of ethics, collaboration, and resource management.
A reflection on implications for future knowledge pursuits and evolving reconciliation methods would strengthen the essayâs final insight.
The conclusion does not offer a definitive stance or synthesized answer to the prescribed title; consider articulating a clear resolution.

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
